The world has changed, and so has the way information spreads. I used to rely on face-to-face conversations, on what I saw with my own eyes, and on the quiet wisdom passed down through generations. Now, I see images of perfect lives, of people living in luxury, of events that never happened. These things feed my anxiety because I know they are not real. I don’t need to be distracted by what others post; I have my garden, my baking, my climbing. I manage fake content by limiting my time online, only visiting once or twice a week, and always asking myself: Is this something I would see in real life? Does it make sense? response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend roughly a few times weekly on social media

response_parsed.opinion: As a retired man from Bologna who has spent most of his life building things with his hands—whether it’s garden beds or furniture—I find it difficult to trust what I see on social media when it comes to truth. The world has changed, and so has the way information spreads. I used to rely on face-to-face conversations, on what I saw with my own eyes, and on the quiet wisdom passed down through generations. Now, I see images of perfect lives, of people living in luxury, of events that never happened. These things feed my anxiety because I know they are not real. I don’t need to be distracted by what others post; I have my garden, my baking, my climbing. I manage fake content by limiting my time online, only visiting once or twice a week, and always asking myself: Is this something I would see in real life? Does it make sense? Does it align with what I know to be true? I also avoid sharing anything that feels too emotional or exaggerated. My Christian faith teaches me to seek truth and to be cautious of deception. I believe in discernment—like a gardener who checks the soil before planting. If something doesn’t grow, it’s not meant to be. I trust my senses, my memory, and my inner peace more than a screen full of curated lies.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is a retired, conscientious, and anxious man with a strong sense of reality and values rooted in tradition and personal experience. His low openness and high conscientiousness mean he values order, accuracy, and stability. Given his background in hands-on work and religious beliefs, he relies on tangible reality and moral judgment. The anxiety makes him sensitive to uncertainty, which is why fake content triggers emotional distress. Therefore, managing fake content involves minimizing exposure, using personal judgment, and grounding decisions in lived experience and faith. The solution aligns with his personality traits and life values.
citations: personal experience of a retired manual worker
